preFix.title: Brown soda bread
preFix.subtitle: Traditional Irish wholemeal soda bread baked in one loaf
preFix.excerpt: Brown soda bread is the most widely made bread in Ireland. It requires no yeast and no kneading: wholemeal flour, bicarbonate of soda, buttermilk, and salt mixed into a shaggy dough, shaped quickly, and baked. The result is dense, slightly nutty, and very good toasted with butter.
preFix.sourceNotes: Traditional Irish soda bread. Brown soda bread using wholemeal flour is one of the most fundamental Irish baked goods, eaten across the country at breakfast and with soup. The use of bicarbonate of soda as a leavening agent dates from the early 19th century, when baking soda became available in Ireland and quickly became the standard raising agent for household bread.

content.text: Brown soda bread is one of the fastest homemade breads you can make. There is no yeast, no proving, and almost no technique beyond mixing and shaping. The key is not to overwork the dough: mix until it just comes together, shape it into a round, cut a deep cross, and put it straight into the oven. The acid in the buttermilk reacts with the bicarbonate of soda immediately, so the bread must go into the oven quickly after mixing.
Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Mix both flours, the bicarbonate of soda, and salt in a large bowl. Make a well in the centre.

Pour in the buttermilk and mix quickly with your hand or a wooden spoon until a shaggy, slightly sticky dough forms. Do not knead. Add a splash more buttermilk if the dough is too dry to come together.
Turn onto a lightly floured baking tray or sheet. Pat into a round about 4 cm tall. Cut a deep cross through the top, almost to the base, to allow it to expand as it bakes.
Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until the crust is dark brown and the loaf sounds hollow when tapped on the bottom. Rest for 15 minutes before slicing.

items.fix: Check the bicarbonate of soda is in date and put the loaf into the oven immediately after shaping.
items.cause: Bicarbonate of soda is old, or the dough sat too long before baking.
items.symptom: The bread is dense and does not rise

items.fix: If the crust is darkening too fast, cover loosely with foil. Check by tapping: a hollow sound means it is done.
items.cause: Too high a temperature, or loaf not baked long enough.
items.symptom: The outside is very dark but the inside is gummy

items.fix: Add buttermilk until the dough is sticky enough that it holds together but does not pour. Soda bread is naturally denser than yeasted bread.
items.cause: Too much flour or not enough buttermilk.
items.symptom: The bread is dry and crumbly

content.text: Soda bread has been made in Ireland since the 1840s, when bicarbonate of soda became available as a cheap and reliable raising agent. It suited the Irish domestic kitchen: no oven thermometer was needed, no yeast had to be maintained, and the bread could be made and baked in under an hour. Brown soda bread using wholemeal flour is the most common form, eaten at breakfast spread with butter and at lunch alongside soup. It is one of the most recognisable elements of Irish food culture.

initialFindings.message: tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
